did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper (Abstract BT09) evaluated the clinical performance of an AI-based medical device (AIaMD) deployed in an urgent suspected skin cancer pathway. The AI system was used for screening, triage, and assessment of skin lesions suspicious for skin cancer, with results including sensitivity (100% for melanoma, 99.5% for all skin cancer) and specificity (55.9% for benign lesions) derived from the AI's assessments.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The provided text contains Abstract BT09, titled 'Clinical performance of an artificial intelligence-based medical device deployed within an urgent suspected skin cancer pathway'. The abstract describes a 'prospective, postdeployment, single-centre clinical performance review' (a type of original research/validation study) evaluating the sensitivity and specificity of an AI device on 1363 lesions. Although the document is a collection of abstracts from a supplement, the specific content for BT09 represents original experimental/observational research results rather than a narrative or systematic review of prior literature.

title: Clinical performance of an artificial intelligence-based medical device deployed within an urgent suspected skin cancer pathway
brief_summary: This study evaluated the clinical performance of an AI-based medical device (AIaMD) deployed in an urgent suspected skin cancer pathway at a UK secondary care trust. It was a prospective, postdeployment, single-centre review assessing 1363 lesions from October 2021 to October 2022, with outcomes confirmed by histology or teledermatology. The AIaMD demonstrated high sensitivity for melanoma (100%) and all skin cancers (99.5%), along with a benign lesion specificity of 55.9%. The negative predictive value was 99.8%, and the conversion rate for high-risk lesions to melanoma or SCC diagnosis was 13.8%. The service discharged 9.4% of patients without using dermatologist capacity, suggesting AI can improve referral accuracy and reduce burden.

lesion_types_studied.details: 51 melanomas were confirmed by histology reports; AIaMD sensitivity for melanoma was 100% (n = 51/51).
64 squamous cell carcinomas were confirmed by histology reports; included in the 'all skin cancer' group (218 total).
96 basal cell carcinomas were confirmed by histology reports; included in the 'all skin cancer' group (218 total).
8 rare skin cancers were confirmed by histology reports; included in the 'all skin cancer' group (218 total).
244 premalignancies were confirmed by histology reports; AIaMD sensitivity for premalignancy was 96.3% (n = 235/244).
777 benign/noncancerous lesions were confirmed by histology or teledermatology; AIaMD specificity for benign lesions was 55.9% (n = 434/777).

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnosis (melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, rare skin cancer, premalignancy, or benign lesion)
all_task_types.details: The AI-based medical device (AIaMD) was used for screening, triage, and assessment of skin lesions suspicious for skin cancer. The study evaluated the device's ability to classify lesions into discrete diagnostic categories, reporting sensitivity for melanoma (100%), all skin cancers (99.5%), and premalignancy (96.3%), as well as specificity for benign lesions (55.9%). The AI outputted a classification (e.g., high risk vs. premalignant/benign) which was compared against histology or teledermatology confirmation.

datasets_used.name: Urgent Suspected Skin Cancer (USCR) Pathway Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective, postdeployment, single-centre clinical performance review cohort from a UK secondary care trust (West Suffolk NHS Foundation Trust). It consists of patients referred via an urgent suspected skin cancer pathway from primary care between October 2021 and October 2022. Outcomes were confirmed by histology reports for cancerous lesions and histology reports or Consultant teledermatology assessment for noncancerous lesions.

use.testing: Used for clinical performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, NPV) of the AI-based medical device (AIaMD) in a real-world deployment setting.
use.other: The AIaMD was deployed as a decision-support tool in this pathway; the study serves as a postmarket service evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study reports descriptive clinical performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, NPV, conversion rate) without statistical significance testing.

Urgent Suspected Skin Cancer (USCR) Path.notes: The study was a prospective, postdeployment, single-centre clinical performance review conducted from October 2021 to October 2022. Outcomes were confirmed by histology reports for cancerous lesions and histology reports or Consultant teledermatology assessment for noncancerous lesions. The local clinical commissioning group conversion rate for 2020–2021 was 9.9%.

architecture_details.versions: Two most recent versions deployed between October 2021 and October 2022

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Screening, triage, and assessment of skin lesions suspicious for skin cancer

conclusions: The AI-based medical device (AIaMD) demonstrated high sensitivity for melanoma (100%) and all skin cancers (99.5%), as well as high specificity for benign lesions (55.9%) in a prospective, postdeployment, single-centre clinical performance review.
The AIaMD pathway achieved a negative predictive value of 99.8%, indicating its ability to correctly rule out cancer in lesions labelled as premalignant or benign.

The integration of AIaMD improved the conversion rate of urgent suspected skin cancer referrals to melanoma or squamous cell carcinoma diagnoses (13.8%) compared to the local clinical commissioning group baseline (9.9%).

The service discharged 9.4% of patients without using trust dermatologist capacity, with a potential discharge rate of 18.3% if a second read by a consultant dermatologist was not required, suggesting AI can reduce the burden of face-to-face referrals.
Areas for improvement identified include ensuring referrers provide concise history and clinical impressions, using gel for dermatoscopic images, and providing feedback on image quality.
Future work may consider establishing photographic hubs to standardize referral photographic quality.
important_remarks: The study is a single-centre, postdeployment clinical performance review, limiting generalizability compared to multi-centre or pre-deployment validation studies.
There is a significant conflict of interest: three of the four authors are employed by Skin Analytics, the provider of the AI device, which may introduce bias.
The AI device is a UK Conformity Assessed Class IIa-approved medical device, indicating regulatory clearance but not necessarily independent clinical efficacy validation.

The study reports high sensitivity (100% for melanoma, 99.5% for all skin cancers) but moderate specificity (55.9% for benign lesions), suggesting a trade-off favoring cancer detection over reducing unnecessary referrals.

The conversion rate for high-risk lesions to melanoma or SCC diagnosis (13.8%) was higher than the local baseline (9.9%), but the absolute number of cancers detected is small (n=218), which may affect statistical robustness.
The study acknowledges limitations in data quality, including poor image quality and referrals containing multiple lesions, which can cause confusion for the AI system.
The study does not report confidence intervals or p-values for the performance metrics, limiting the assessment of statistical precision.

The AI system was used as a decision-support tool with a mandatory second read by a consultant dermatologist for all cases, meaning the AI did not autonomously make final diagnostic or triage decisions in this deployment.
